description.task: Write a Java program that demonstrates 6 commonly used String methods on a given sentence.
instructions: Call trim() and print 'Trimmed: Java Programming is Fun!'
Call toUpperCase() on the trimmed string and print 'Upper: JAVA PROGRAMMING IS FUN!'
Call toLowerCase() on the trimmed string and print 'Lower: java programming is fun!'
Call replace("Fun", "Amazing") and print 'Replaced: Java Programming is Amazing!'
description.hint: Chain method calls: String trimmed = sentence.trim(); then call methods on trimmed. String.length() counts characters including spaces.
description.expectedOutput:
Trimmed: Java Programming is Fun!
Upper: JAVA PROGRAMMING IS FUN!
Lower: java programming is fun!
Length (trimmed): 25
Contains 'Java': true
Replaced: Java Programming is Amazing!

description.task: Demonstrate the difference between == (reference equality) and .equals() (content equality) for Strings in Java.
description.context: A common Java bug is using == to compare Strings. The == operator compares references (memory addresses), while .equals() compares actual character content.
instructions: Print 's1 == s2: true' (both point to the same String pool literal).
Print 's1 == s3: false' (s3 is a new object in heap, different reference).
The equalsIgnoreCase line is already provided — do not change it.
description.hint: String literals are interned (pooled) so s1 == s2 is true. new String(...) always creates a new heap object so s1 == s3 is false.
description.expectedOutput:
s1 == s2: true
s1 == s3: false
s1.equals(s2): true
s1.equals(s3): true
s1.equalsIgnoreCase(s4): true

description.task: Use StringBuilder to build a comma-separated list of numbers from 1 to 10, then demonstrate why StringBuilder is preferred over String concatenation in loops.
description.context: Strings in Java are immutable. Concatenating in a loop with + creates a new String object each time, wasting memory. StringBuilder mutates in-place and is much more efficient.
instructions: Inside the loop, append the number, and if i < 10 also append ", ".
Call sb.reverse() and print the reversed string.
Do NOT change the println statements outside the loop.
description.hint: sb.append(i); if (i < 10) sb.append(", "); — The reversed output flips the character sequence including commas and digits.
